elements.sent-2411-001: As large language models (LLMs) rapidly advance and integrate into daily life, the privacy risks they pose are attracting increasing attention.
elements.sent-2411-002: We focus on a specific privacy risk where LLMs may help identify the authorship of anonymous texts, which challenges the effectiveness of anonymity in real-world systems such as anonymous peer review systems.
elements.sent-2411-003: To investigate these risks, we present AIDBench, a new benchmark that incorporates several author identification datasets, including emails, blogs, reviews, articles, and research papers.
elements.sent-2411-004: AIDBench utilizes two evaluation methods: one-to-one authorship identification, which determines whether two texts are from the same author; and one-to-many authorship identification, which, given a query text and a list of candidate texts, identifies the candidate most likely written by the same author as the query text.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also introduce a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)-based method to enhance the large-scale authorship identification capabilities of LLMs, particularly when input lengths exceed the models' context windows, thereby establishing a new baseline for authorship identification using LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our experiments with AIDBench demonstrate that LLMs can correctly guess authorship at rates well above random chance, revealing new privacy risks posed by these powerful models.
elements.sent-2411-007: The source code and data will be made publicly available after acceptance.
elements.sent-2411-008: Large language models (LLMs) have experienced widespread application~\citep{zhao2023survey, chang2024survey due to remarkable capabilities in processing and generating human-like text.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Specifically, they are able to follow human instructions~\citep{aw2023instruction to accomplish various tasks in both professional and personal spheres.}
elements.sent-2411-010: At the same time, the wide adoption of LLMs also brings impact on societal paradigms that cannot be overlooked~\citep{huang2023survey, chen2024pandora, chen2024mj .}
elements.sent-2411-011: This paper delves into one such challenge posed by LLMs: the issue of privacy risks~\citep{yang2024memorization, zheng2024permllm, li2023privacy, li2023p .}
elements.sent-2411-012: In the study of privacy risks associated with language models, researchers primarily focus on the leakage of private information in the training data~\citep{li2023multi, nasr2023scalable, wen2023last .}
elements.sent-2411-013: As we navigate through a world where information is increasingly digitized and interconnected, the potential for privacy breaches becomes more diverse and realistic.
elements.sent-2411-014: Specifically, we explore how large models, through their advanced capabilities and extensive knowledge base, can identify anonymous text based on the context and content at hand.
elements.sent-2411-015: Due to exceptional text analysis capabilities, LLMs can be used to infer private information and considered as a tool to help individuals conduct privacy attacks~\citep{staab2023beyond and infer the author's information of anonymous texts~\citep{nyffenegger2023anonymity .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: This kind of privacy breach is well motivated by the real-world application of anonymous systems.
elements.sent-2411-017: Systems such as academic peer reviews\footnote{\url{https://openreview.net , student confession websites, and corporate employee anonymous communication platforms, are designed to protect the identity of individuals~\citep{edman2009anonymity, hohenberger2014anonize , ensuring that their comments or concerns can be shared without fear of retribution or bias.}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, the integrity of these systems is highly dependent on the effectiveness of their anonymity.
elements.sent-2411-019: The anonymity of these systems is under threat if the text produced within them can be used to accurately identify the author.
elements.sent-2411-020: For instance, consider the academic peer review system, where anonymity is a cornerstone of the process~\citep{panadero2019empirical, coomber2010effect .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Typically, within a conference, a reviewer may be assigned with evaluating multiple papers, and the reviewer is given a random identifier when they write a review for a paper~\citep{ragone2013peer, zhang2022system .}
elements.sent-2411-022: A reviewer may participate in multiple events over a period of time.
elements.sent-2411-023: People usually cannot link the random identifiers of a single reviewer in nature.
elements.sent-2411-024: However, equipped with powerful large language models, one may be able to identify a reviewer based on the careful comparison between the content of a review and that of other reviews.
elements.sent-2411-025: If the above process were achieved with high probability, it would pose a significant de-anonymization risk in current systems.
elements.sent-2411-026: In literature, a closely related problem is authorship attribution (AA)~\citep{neal2017surveying,stamatatos2009survey, barlas2020cross .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Usually, there is a list of suspects and a collection of texts with confirmed authorship for each individual on the list.
elements.sent-2411-028: The objective is to determine the author of texts whose authorship is in question, a task that can be divided into three distinct categories: closed-set attribution where the list of suspects is assumed to contain the actual author of the disputed texts, open-set attribution where the true author may not be included in the initial list of suspects, and author verification where the disputed text is compared against a single candidate author and verify whether this one individual is indeed the author of the text in question.
elements.sent-2411-029: Specifically, \citet{Huang2024CanLL employ LLMs to perform authorship attribution with carefully designed prompts on two datasets open-source blog~\citep{Schler2006EffectsOA and email datasets~\citep{klimt2004enron .}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: A recent survey \cite{huang2024authorship summarizes recent progress and available datasets for authorship attribution, and extend the landscape to LLM-generated text detection and attribution.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Moreover, it is worthy to mention that PAN is a series of scientific events with datasets and competitions focusing on digital text forensics and stylometry, specifically cross-topic and cross-genre authorship verification \citep{stamatatos2022overview , author identification and multi-author analysis \citep{bevendorff2023overview , and multi-author writing style analysis and generative AI authorship verification \citep{bevendorff2024overview .}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: However, these setups differ significantly from the challenging anonymous review systems where one has to compare hundreds of texts without effective author profiles available.
elements.sent-2411-033: In this paper, we introduce AIDBench, a comprehensive benchmark designed for extensive and in-depth authorship identification.
elements.sent-2411-034: The goal is to identify texts written by the same author when a specific text is known to belong to that person; specifically, given a disputed text, our task is to find candidate texts most likely authored by the same individual.
elements.sent-2411-035: To systematically study this problem, we collect multiple datasets, including research papers, Enron emails~\citep{klimt2004enron , blogs~\citep{Schler2006EffectsOA , Gaudian articles~\citep{guardian and IMDb reviews~\cite{Seroussi2011AuthorshipAW whose features are illustrated in Table~\ref{tab:dataset_summary .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: For the research papers dataset, each entry includes the title, abstract and introduction, and all of them are computer science papers gathered from arXiv\footnote{\url{https://arxiv.org , along with their ground-truth authorship information.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: We evaluate the capability of LLMs to accomplish this task under various setups, directly prompting both commercial platforms\footnote{We have not tested all commercial models due to availability and cost constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Moreover, as the number of texts increases and exceeds the context window of LLMs, we propose a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)-based~\citep{lewis2020retrieval, gao2023retrieval, ding2024survey, zhao2024retrieval attribution method to address situations where the models cannot accommodate all the information even with context extension~\citep{jin2024llm, ding2024longrope, zhang2024extending .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: The contributions of AIDBench are summarized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-040: AIDBench presents a novel dataset of research papers specifically designed to evaluate the authorship identification capabilities of LLMs in academic writing.
elements.sent-2411-041: Using AIDBench, we conduct extensive experiments of authorship identification with mainstream LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-042: These experiments are particularly performed under stringent conditions without any author profile information.
elements.sent-2411-043: Our empirical findings demonstrate that the potential of LLMs as tools for privacy breaches is evident and cannot be ignored.
elements.sent-2411-044: Additionally, we propose a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)-based methodological pipeline for evaluating the authorship identification capabilities of LLMs for large-scale authorship identification.
elements.sent-2411-045: In this section, we provide a detailed description of AIDBench.
elements.sent-2411-046: We begin by presenting an overview of the benchmark, outlining the pipeline for authorship identification using large language models (LLMs).
elements.sent-2411-047: We then describe the datasets used in our benchmark, the evaluation tasks conducted, and the metrics employed for assessment.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Outline of authorship identification with LLMs
elements.sent-2411-048: Figure \ref{fig:AIDBench provides an overview of our proposed AIDBench framework.}
elements.sent-2411-049: We begin by selecting a dataset for evaluation, such as the Research Paper dataset.
elements.sent-2411-050: From this dataset, we sample a subset of texts from several authors, randomly selecting one as the Target Text and designating the remaining texts as candidates.
elements.sent-2411-051: These texts are then incorporated into an authorship identification prompt, which is presented to the LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-052: The models generate responses indicating which candidate texts are more likely authored by the same individual as the Target Text.
elements.sent-2411-053: We repeat this process multiple times to obtain average performance metrics for the task.
elements.sent-2411-054: Finally, we employ metrics such as precision, recall, and rank to provide a clear and intuitive assessment of the LLMs' capabilities.

elements.sent-2411-055: AIDBench comprises five datasets: Research Paper, Enron Email, Blog, IMDb Review, and Guardian.
elements.sent-2411-056: In this subsection, we provide detailed descriptions of each dataset.. Research Paper.
elements.sent-2411-057: This newly collected dataset consists of research papers posted on arXiv under the CS.LG tag (the field of machine learning in the computer science domain) from 2019 to 2024.
elements.sent-2411-058: After removing duplicate entries and authors with fewer than ten papers, the dataset includes 24,095 papers from 1,500 authors, ensuring that each author has at least ten papers.
elements.sent-2411-059: We use this dataset to investigate their potential privacy risks when using LLMs to identify authorship of academic writing.. Enron Email.
elements.sent-2411-060: The Enron email dataset contains approximately 500,000 emails generated by employees of the Enron Corporation.
elements.sent-2411-061: For our benchmark, similar to~\citet{Huang2024CanLL , we removed sender and receiver information and discarded short emails.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Ultimately, we retained 174 authors, each with 50 emails.. Blog.
elements.sent-2411-063: The Blog Authorship Corpus~\citep{Schler2006EffectsOA comprises the collected posts of 19,320 bloggers gathered from Blogger.com\footnote{\url{https://www.blogger.com in August 2004.}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: We selected 1,500 authors from the dataset, each with 10 blog posts.
elements.sent-2411-065: The content of this dataset is closely related to daily life, providing linguistic characteristics, writing styles, word usage habits, and special characters that can be used to infer an author's identity.. IMDb Review.
elements.sent-2411-066: The IMDb review data are selected from the IMDb62 dataset~\citep{Seroussi2011AuthorshipAW .}
elements.sent-2411-067: After filtering out reviews with fewer than 10 words, we randomly retained 50 reviews for each of the 62 authors in our dataset.. Guardian.
elements.sent-2411-068: The Guardian corpus dataset~\citep{guardian is designed to explore cross-genre and cross-topic authorship attribution.}
elements.sent-2411-069: The corpus comprises texts published in The Guardian daily newspaper, mainly opinion articles (comments).
elements.sent-2411-070: It includes articles from 13 authors across five topics—politics, society, UK, world, and books—and retains 50 articles per author.
elements.sent-2411-071: As illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:AIDBench , we outline the evaluation tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Each task involves a query text and a set of candidate texts, with the objective of identifying which candidates are most likely authored by the same individual as the query text.
elements.sent-2411-073: Based on the number of candidate texts, we introduce the following specific tasks.. One-to-one identification.
elements.sent-2411-074: Commonly known as authorship verification, this task involves a single candidate text and aims to determine whether the text pair is authored by the same person, forming a binary classification problem.
elements.sent-2411-075: In our evaluation, we prompt the LLM with: ``Here are a pair of texts: [Text Pair].
elements.sent-2411-076: Determine if they belong to the same author.''.
elements.sent-2411-077: More effective prompts or additional information can be provided to the LLM to achieve more accurate and reasonable responses.
elements.sent-2411-078: For instance, \cite{Hung2023WhoWI employs a Chain-of-Thought prompt and a series of intermediate reasoning steps to significantly enhance the LLMs' authorship verification ability.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Similarly, as shown in \cite{Huang2024CanLL and \cite{Hung2023WhoWI , instructing LLMs to analyze texts based on writing style, linguistic characteristics, and word usage habits, rather than content and topics, can further improve authorship verification.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Due to the subjective nature of the one-to-one identification task and its heavy reliance on the intrinsic judgment of LLMs, it is natural to consider a scenario involving two candidate texts requiring a comparative decision.
elements.sent-2411-081: The objective here is to determine which candidate text is more likely authored by the same individual as the query text; we refer to this as the one-to-two identification task.
elements.sent-2411-082: Due to space limitations, we defer the task design, metrics, and experimental results for the one-to-two identification task to Appendix \ref{app:1to2 .. One-to-many identification.}
elements.sent-2411-083: In this setup, we provide a number of candidate texts and ask LLMs to determine texts that are most likely authored by the same person as the text in the query.
elements.sent-2411-084: This is different from the usual closed-set authorship attribution task \citep{Huang2024CanLL , where multiple authors and their writings are provided in the context of LLMs, and the task is to attribute a target text to one of these authors.}
elements.sent-2411-085: We do not provide any authorship information to LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-086: Instead in our experiments, we randomly sample a number of authors and put all of their writings into a set.
elements.sent-2411-087: Then one specific text is chosen at random as the target text while the rest from the set form the candidate texts.
elements.sent-2411-088: The task is to ask LLMs to identify the texts in the candidate set that are mostly likely authored by the same person as the target text, ranking the results by confidence scores.
elements.sent-2411-089: The performance of one-to-many authorship identification tasks heavily depends on the ability of LLMs to handle long contexts.
elements.sent-2411-090: Current commercial LLMs support relatively large context windows; for example, GPT-4-Turbo can process contexts up to 128,000 tokens, and Kimi supports up to 2 million tokens.
elements.sent-2411-091: In contrast, open-source models like Llama-3-8B-Instruct\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct support context windows of only up to 8,000 tokens.}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Moreover, the authorship identification task relies heavily on the LLMs' capacity for high-level comprehension of the context, which becomes increasingly challenging as the length of the context grows.
elements.sent-2411-093: To address this limitation, we propose a simple Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)-based method, as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:RAG .}
elements.sent-2411-094: In this approach, we first use pre-trained embedding models like sentence-transformers to encode both the target text and the candidate texts.
elements.sent-2411-095: We then calculate the similarities between the target text and each candidate, selecting only the top k texts with the highest similarity scores.
elements.sent-2411-096: These top k texts are provided to the LLM, thereby reducing the context length and enabling more effective processing.
elements.sent-2411-097: The motivation behind this method is that although embedding models tend to focus more on the semantic meanings of texts than on their linguistic characteristics, we can still use the similarities they provide to filter out texts that are significantly dissimilar to the target text.
elements.sent-2411-098: This approach ensures that the total length of the candidate texts remains within the context windows of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-099: For the one-to-one identification task, LLMs are required to classify whether a pair of texts were written by the same author by answering ``True'' or ``False''.
elements.sent-2411-100: While accuracy can serve as an evaluation metric, it may not fully capture performance due to potential class imbalances in the dataset, for instance, when there are many negative pairs compared to positive pairs, or vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-101: Therefore, we adopt Precision, Recall as our evaluation criteria to assess the performance of LLMs under various setups more comprehensively.
elements.sent-2411-102: To be specific, Precision and Recall are defined as follows: \begin{equation}\text{Precision} = \frac{\#\text{True Positive Pairs}}{\#\text{Predicted Positive Pairs} }; \quad\quad \text{Recall} = \frac{\#\text{True Positive Pairs}}{\#\text{Positive Pairs} }.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}Precision = \frac{\#True Positive Pairs}{\#Predicted Positive Pairs }; \quad\quad Recall = \frac{\#True Positive Pairs}{\#Positive Pairs }.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-103: For the one-to-many identification task, we employ multiple evaluation metrics to capture the problem's complex nature.
elements.sent-2411-104: In our experiments, we instruct the models to rank candidate texts based on confidence scores and repeat the experiments using multiple target texts.
elements.sent-2411-105: We utilize metrics such as Rank@1 and Rank@5 in our benchmark, where Rank@x indicates that at least one text authored by the same individual as the target text is found within the top x ranked texts.
elements.sent-2411-106: Additionally, since we expect the LLMs to select as many correct candidate texts as possible, we use Precision@x as a metric, where Precision@x denotes the proportion of correct predictions among the top x texts.
elements.sent-2411-107: In this section, we perform authorship identification tasks with the above introduced datasets on popular LLMs, e.g., GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4-turbo, Claude-3.5-sonnet, Kimi, Baichuan2-Turbo-192k, and Qwen1.5-72B-Chat\footnote{\url{https://huggingface.co/Qwen/Qwen1.5-72B-Chat .}}
elements.sent-2411-108: In the following, we present two tasks (one-to-one and one-to-many identification tasks) on two representative datasets (Guardian and Research Paper), respectively.
elements.sent-2411-109: Other setups and results are deferred to Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-110: As previously mentioned, the Guardian dataset categorizes text data by both author and topic.
elements.sent-2411-111: Leveraging this, we design a more challenging scenario to evaluate the one-to-one identification capabilities of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-112: Specifically, we randomly select pairs of texts as positive examples where each pair is authored by the same individual but covers different topics.
elements.sent-2411-113: For negative examples, we sample text pairs that share the same topic but are written by different authors.
elements.sent-2411-114: We believe this setup provides a robust evaluation of LLM capabilities and offers deeper insights into their potential.
elements.sent-2411-115: For datasets other than the Guardian, we simply sample text pairs where the positive examples consist of texts by the same author, and the negative examples consist of texts by different authors.
elements.sent-2411-116: We conduct experiments using three different random seeds, recording the precision and recall, and reporting the mean and standard deviation of these metrics.
elements.sent-2411-117: The total number of examples is set to 200, and we perform experiments with positive-to-negative example ratios of 150/50, 100/100, and 50/150, respectively.. \renewcommand{\arraystretch {1.05.}}

elements.sent-2411-118: As shown in Table~\ref{exp:one-to-one guardian , our evaluation results on the Guardian dataset indicate that precision decreases as the proportion of positive examples decreases.}
elements.sent-2411-119: This suggests that all the models we evaluated tend to predict many negative examples as positive.
elements.sent-2411-120: In scenarios where there are fewer positive examples than negative ones, the performance of LLMs on the Guardian dataset is even worse than random guessing.
elements.sent-2411-121: The Baichuan model performs the worst, with precision close to the proportion of positive examples in the dataset and recall near 1, indicating that it almost considers all text pairs to be written by the same author.
elements.sent-2411-122: In fact, under the 50/150 setting, the LLMs encounter significant failures in evaluation on many other datasets (results are shown in Appendix \ref{app:one_to_one ).}
elements.sent-2411-123: These evaluation results differ significantly from the conclusions of prior works such as \citep{Huang2024CanLL and \citep{Hung2023WhoWI .}}
elements.sent-2411-124: However, we still find that GPT-4-turbo exhibits very good precision on all datasets other than the Guardian, which we attribute to its superior reasoning capabilities compared to other models.
elements.sent-2411-125: This indicates that as LLMs continue to develop, they have the potential to become even more powerful tools for de-anonymization.
elements.sent-2411-126: We believe that previous evaluation methods are not sufficiently rigorous and propose the following two points: First, the evaluation of LLMs' one-to-one identification capability should be conducted under cross-topic conditions, for which the Guardian dataset provides an excellent benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-127: Second, the proportion of negative examples in the dataset should be varied to assess whether LLMs incorrectly classify many negative examples as positive.
elements.sent-2411-128: In this section, we evaluate the one-to-many identification capabilities of LLMs with the Research Paper dataset.
elements.sent-2411-129: We consider three scenarios: 2 Authors with 20 papers; 5 Authors with 50 papers; and 10 Authors with 100 papers.. \renewcommand{\arraystretch {1.05.}}

elements.sent-2411-165: In this work, we propose a benchmark to evaluate the authorship identification capabilities of large language models (LLMs).
elements.sent-2411-166: Specifically, we have collected a new dataset, referred to as the ``Research Paper'' dataset, to identify authorship based solely on the content of academic papers.
elements.sent-2411-167: By combining this with various existing public datasets, we have curated a comprehensive benchmark dataset.
elements.sent-2411-168: We evaluate the authorship identification task using multiple models and services, revealing potential privacy risks in real-world anonymous systems when employing LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-169: To enable authorship identification of long texts, we have devised a pipeline utilizing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG).
elements.sent-2411-170: This approach allows us to tailor the evaluation of LLMs with different contextual capacities to adapt to the task.
elements.sent-2411-171: Further discussion on limitations and future directions is deferred to Appendix \ref{app:limitation .}
elements.sent-2411-172: Our benchmark is related to the longstanding authorship attribution task \citep{stamatatos2009survey , which is initially formulated as supervised authorship identification and recently addressed with LLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-173: Early work uses text statistics such as n-grams, topic, or syntax as features~\citep{Koppel2007MeasuringDU, Sharma2018AnIO, Sundararajan2018WhatR, SeroussiTopicModel , and then leverage SVM, random forest or topic models to conduct authorship verification.}
elements.sent-2411-174: Some work uses deep learning models like RNNs and CNNs to extract more complex features for authorship verification~\citep{Bagnall2015AuthorIU, benzebouchi2018multi, Ruder2016CharacterlevelAM .}
elements.sent-2411-175: Pretrained models such as BERT are also used to get text embeddings~\citep{HuertasTato2022PARTPA, Barlas2020CrossDomainAA, Fabien2020BertAAB, rivera-soto-etal-2021-learning and one can verify the authorship of two texts by measuring the similarity of their embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-176: Due to the abilities of LLMs understanding texts and human instructions, researchers naturally explore using LLMs for authorship identification.
elements.sent-2411-177: The guidance includes ignoring topics, ignoring content, and focusing on linguistic features.
elements.sent-2411-178: Nonetheless, they did not experiment with hard scenarios such as the same author with different topics, or different authors with the same topic.
elements.sent-2411-179: Our paper extends previous work in several key aspects.
elements.sent-2411-180: We focus on an authorship identification task that mimics the anonymous paper review system by identifying the most likely texts from the same author.
elements.sent-2411-181: Additionally, our experiments cover a wider range of scenarios and demonstrate the behaviors of LLMs in various axes, including different context lengths and challenging scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-182: The previous task heavily relies on the intrinsic judgment of LLMs due to its subjective nature.
elements.sent-2411-183: Therefore, we consider a scenario with two candidate texts, requiring a comparative decision.
elements.sent-2411-184: The objective is to determine which candidate text is more likely authored by the same person as the query text.
elements.sent-2411-185: Specifically, we prompt the LLM with, ``Here are two candidate texts: [candidate texts] and a target text [text in query].
elements.sent-2411-186: Determine which candidate text is more likely to be authored by the same person as the target text''.
elements.sent-2411-187: Similar to the previous task, the prompts can be augmented with additional information.
elements.sent-2411-188: We choose GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4-turbo, Kimi and Baichuan2-turbo-192k to perform one-to-two identification on 4 datasets: Guardian, Enron Email, Blog and IMDb Review.
elements.sent-2411-189: We use three different random seeds to repeat our experiments and choose accuracy as the evaluation metric.
elements.sent-2411-190: In this task, each data example consists of three texts: besides the target text, there are two candidate texts.
elements.sent-2411-191: One of the candidate texts is by the same author as the target text, while the other is not.
elements.sent-2411-192: We sample 200 data examples for our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-193: It is worth noticing that for the Guardian dataset, we ensure that the target text and the candidate text belonging to the same author as the target text are from different topics, while the other candidate text has the same topic as the candidate text.
elements.sent-2411-194: The evaluation results are shown in table \ref{exp:one-to-two .. \renewcommand{\arraystretch {1.1.}}}

elements.sent-2411-196: This indicates that although texts from different authors may be similar in topic, GPT-4-turbo can make reasonable comparisons based on the linguistic characteristics of the texts, thereby identifying the candidate text that truly belongs to the same author as the target text, which demonstrates a considerable privacy threat.
elements.sent-2411-197: Though all four models that we evaluate show effectiveness in this task, there is a significant difference in accuracy between different models, which suggests that there is a considerable variance in the abilities of different models to analyze and compare texts and understand instructions.
elements.sent-2411-198: The AIDBench offers a benchmark for assessing LLMs' authorship identification capabilities in scenarios closer to real-world anonymous review systems, highlighting significant privacy risks associated with anonymous systems.
elements.sent-2411-199: In this section, we address the potential limitations of AIDBench and outline future directions.
elements.sent-2411-200: Firstly, it is worth noting that due to cost constraints, we evaluated only a limited number of models.
elements.sent-2411-201: Additionally, large commercial models are continually emerging and evolving rapidly, such as the Gemini~\citep{team2023gemini , Claude 3~\citep{anthropic2024claude , Llama~\citep{touvron2023llama-1, touvron2023llama, meta2024introducing , Mistral families~\citep{jiang2023mistral , and many others~\citep{zeng2022glm, zhang2022opt .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-202: Therefore, future exploration and evaluation of these models are warranted.
elements.sent-2411-203: Another aspect to consider is that research papers often involve multiple authors, each contributing differently to the paper's writing.
elements.sent-2411-204: It would be intriguing to curate more nuanced subsets of research papers based on author positions, such as a first-author subset.
elements.sent-2411-205: This approach could provide insights into how author position affects the authorship identification problem in research papers.
elements.sent-2411-206: Additionally, in our experiments, we only utilize the abstract information of a paper.
elements.sent-2411-207: However, in the future, incorporating more detailed paper information could lead to a more accurate evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-208: Furthermore, in the evaluation process, we employed only two types of prompts: the general prompt and the topic-ignored prompt.
elements.sent-2411-209: Recognizing the significant impact of prompt quality on model responses, it is essential for a more comprehensive exploration of LLMs' authorship identification capabilities that we develop a more nuanced set of prompts for future evaluation and analysis.
elements.sent-2411-210: Finally, the RAG-based approach we introduced enables LLMs with limited context windows to conduct authorship identification.
elements.sent-2411-211: To ensure the success of authorship identification, it's necessary to limit the candidate list obtained through RAG screening to a specific quantity.
elements.sent-2411-212: Additionally, we often encounter individual texts with considerable lengths.
elements.sent-2411-213: Therefore, the future direction of our work involves exploring methods to decompose long texts into shorter segments, indirectly facilitating comparisons between the original texts by comparing these shorter segments.
elements.sent-2411-214: One approach is to segment the long text into several shorter segments.
elements.sent-2411-215: In the one-to-two identification task, LLMs can compare pairs of these shorter segments, assigning scores during each comparison.
elements.sent-2411-216: Ultimately, scores are weighted and summed based on the length of each segment.
